speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Janis. You want to read it out?

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is February 2, 1995. The survivor's name is Helena Snidman. And I am the interviewer, Janis Raisen. We are in Toronto, Canada. And the-- the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Janis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please introduce yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBS] I'm Helen Snidman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBS] In Warsaw

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about Warsaw? What life was like when you were growing up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My private home life? Or the life in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both. Tell me what your home life first.

speaker: interviewee
text: What time? Before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it started to feel that something change in this situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, let's go back before that. So when you were a little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a little girl. [SOBS] I come from a family of six. And life was not bad. My father was a businessman. And we had a decent life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business did he have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a wholesale fruit and a wholesale of textile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother always helped my father in business. And we were six children at home-- four girls and two boys. And they all gone in the war. [SOBS] I'm-- I'm the only one alive from the whole family. We were 138 people in the family, and everybody perished in the war. I went to school, the public school, to grade six. Then I went to a private school, to a gymnasium. And when the war starts to get close, so that we felt the change in Warsaw life, everyday life, my father opened for me a-- a children's [? wares shop. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just go back a little bit before. Can you tell me a little bit about your public school? A little bit about your classmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I cannot remember that. I cannot tell you anything. I can only tell you that-- how life was at home. We used to employ some people to work for us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And very close I could remember one occasion which I live with it, when it was before Passover, and they were painting our apartment, my grandmother put me on a table. And she was reading from the Bible. She was telling me that-- that it's written in the Bible that will come a time when the whole families will get restore-- perish. That will come a war. That one person from our family going to be alive. That's going to be lucky that a new generation can come. This I lived with that all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else would you like me to tell you? Now, your grandparents lived with you in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just my grandmother, that particular one, which I remember what she read that in the Bible, and told me that story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did she live with you for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I was a very little girl when she read it to me. I was maybe three, four years old. And I remember very vividly that what she wrote. That my children, they know, because I used to tell them that story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you had mentioned before that you grew up in a conservative home.

speaker: interviewee
text: A conservative home. We belonged to a Zionist organization-- The Zionist Revisionist-- which is now in Israel, the Likud party, under the auspice of our leader, Ze'ev Jabotinsky. And Begin was later took over. What he was Prime Minister at time that he was. My father was a Revisionist. My two brothers used to go to Hebrew school to Talmud. [SOBBING] And home life was a decent life til-- til a year, a year and a half to the war. Before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family go to synagogue at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They belonged to two synagogues. One they used to go Saturdays because there was a good counter. And my father, my family, belonged to a synagogue which was through my grandfather. Because I had one grandfather. My father's father was still alive. So he wants very badly them to be active in that synagogue. And my father was a very active person socially. Belonged to a few organizations, [INAUDIBLE]. Let's say Passover came. And some poor people, which we had in Warsaw, a lot of poor people didn't have enough money to make the holiday. My father used to work and help them supply with product. He used to give bushels of fruit. Some other members used to give matzoh, bakers. Some other members, like I had an uncle. What he had a farm from goose and turkey. So he used to give turkey donations. And my father organized that everyone brought something. And they spread between the poor people that they couldn't afford to buy that. My father used to go every Saturday when he was well to the Jewish, like here, the Mount Sinai Hospital, and give food for the poor, sick people. [SOBS] And then he belonged to a very important organization that if somebody dies, and they don't have money to bury, they came to my father. And my father used to collect money to bury those particular person which came to ask to be helped. So my father was a very charitable person, and very active in the community. He was well known. It's a matter of fact that his name was Towja. And he had a-- a second name, Towja [NON-ENGLISH] Towja. This is in Jewish. Towja, make me a favor. In family life, we used to go to school. My mother helped in business, as I mentioned before. We had help at home. We had two maids at home. Because it always a minor to be looked after. My mother wasn't home. So she was looking after that. And it wasn't like here, dishwashers and washing machines. So one Gentile lady by the name of Stefa worked for us for over 17 years til the war. When the war broke out, and they got taken to the ghetto, I was told when I came back to Warsaw that she took them out in bags to her little town, you know. And it looks like from there, they got perish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my oldest sister was Pearla. She was married, and she had that little boy, Jurek. She was married for a prominent man. He was a charter accountant. And he was a journalist. He used to write [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBS] for people like here the Canadian Jews. And under the direction of Bialik. And I used to belong to Bialik's organization, which we used to gathered and write they used to call small paper for young people. They used to write articles, let's say, what went down in school and what-- what experience you had at home from the week. And well, naturally, we have to make homework. I was very slow in French, so I had a special tutor for teach me French. My father was very interested in education, so he took French, too. And he spoke perfect Russian, read Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else can I tell you? I had a sister, Malka. She was married. She had a little boy. And I forgot his name. And your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, two brother. Mendel was the oldest one, like I say. They used to go to parochial school. Like here, [INAUDIBLE] it called in Warsaw. And they went to school. They-- they played golf. One belong to the Maccabi, was playing hockey. [SOBBING] And they now had a little sister, Rena, which I could [? savor ?] because I came back when my father didn't allow her to come with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the last time I went away from Warsaw to Bialystok, she took me to the train with my mother. [SOBS] And I had on my hand, my mother grabbed her. She started crying. If I leave the little girl, your father will kill me, and won't let me in the house. So I let it go. She was with me. And on the second step on the train. And what else should I tell you? What you said when you were in high school, you belonged to Betar? Tell me a little bit more-- Well you did-- you didn't have to know. I belonged to Betar before-- long before I was in high school. Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I say, my father used to be a Revisionist, too. And I belonged to Betar. I had a big portrait [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in our like an office. And when I had that store from the children's [INAUDIBLE] was working a girl. [SOBS] And that girl came. She took down the portrait. And says, I don't want anybody to hurt you. You're too nice of a person. So she took down the portrait. And the-- and my father arranged for me to cross the border to Bialystok Because he had business associate in that region. And the idea was that when I'll go there, that eventually he'll evacuate the whole family. But didn't turn out this way. He was already packed to go to the Russian zone. At that time was that Russian zone-- before Bialystok belonged to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just go back a little bit. I wanted to ask you if you encountered a lot of anti-Semitism growing up in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, sure, sure. They used to have a slogan. Can I say in Polish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] . That means, the girls is with us, but the Jews, the men, we are against that. They used to have a slogan. [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] Is female. And that was the reason I didn't go any longer to school. And my-- my father opened that textile-- that children's wares shop. Because it was no use of continue school. They-- they were throwing stones on Jews at the university.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me a little bit about the store that your father opened up for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The store. Well like I say, there was no use of going any further to school, so my father opened up a children's wares shop for me, which I carry on not too long, because the war broke out. And when it close to the war, when it broke out, you had to put on a [NON-ENGLISH] in the front of the door. And it was Helena's Shop. I had to change on [? Haja's ?] Shop, to show that it is a Jewish store.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have the store for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For like approximately a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the store was in the same building where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We-- we had other place to live, too. But this was the place the [INAUDIBLE] address. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this when you first opened your store?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year and a half before the war. That means it would be like '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you tell me what it was like just before 1939, and the end of 1938, when you still had your store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I say, we felt in the air the business become bad. Because we felt in the air the old kind of meeting went on. The anti-Semitic meeting. And you wasn't safe to walk in the street at night when it dark. My parents didn't allow us to go now, because you got beaten up. And on account of beaten up, I'll tell you then a story what I witnessed. And then I told my father that I'm leaving Warsaw. That I didn't do anything wrong, [SOBS] that I should stay just because I was born Jewish. I was walking on the street, and the Germans were already in Warsaw. And two young boys, maybe 19, 20 grabbed an Hassidic Jew to the hallway. And I went to look. And I start yelling at them in Yiddish. [NON-ENGLISH]. I didn't imagine that they're going to do. So they shaved a half a face with the beard from that Hassidic Jew. And then when I was walking through the house, I've seen all kind of notes on the side of the street, corners of the street from Warsaw. And in Warsaw, we had big lamps like here outside. It was pasted on with orange writing, and yellow-- 'Jewish swine.' That means pig. 'Jews have to be melted to schmaltz.' That means to fat. And 'All Jews are Communist.' I came to my father. This was six weeks with the Germans. I came to my father, and I say, I won't stay here. I didn't do anything wrong to anybody just because I was born Jewish. [SOBS] I don't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father made fun out of me. Take a look! She wants to be smarter than everybody. So I told him, I'm not smarter. I'm a Revisionist. I'm a [INAUDIBLE]. I don't allow anybody to spit in my face. I want to go. So my father arranged somebody to take me across the border. And I went to see his business associate in Bialystok. What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans were already in Warsaw-- 1939. I was six weeks with the Germans, and then I left. That means it was September, October.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you got to Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got to Bialystok, we used to come together with young people. And we were planning to go to Israel. That this is the only-- at that time was Palestine, not Israel. Because it wasn't an independent state yet. We're speaking 1939. And we friends, we used to come together in cafe houses, and we used to talk. What's going to be the future? How it's going to be. Then I find out that Mosze, my brother, is in Russia, in Lviv. And from Bialystok I went to Lviv to look for him. I find him. And he was with me. And when we crossed the border, the customs to go up to Chernovitz. And from there we were intending to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to back up a little bit. Before you went to meet your brother, how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. Because at that time was Russia took over that part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever questioned about your-- your identity as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. In Russia, they didn't question your identity. But I went. I bought a ticket, and I went. I look for my brother. I find him. Well, what else do you want to question me before that? So later we'll come back to it, how I lost him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in touch with your family at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were still in touch with your family back in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was touching with my family at that time, yes. And I received some news through friends that I've met that they knew my father. On this street they used to recognize me that I am Towja's [INAUDIBLE]. Because apparently I look a lot like my father used to look. So on the street, people used to stop me and tell me, oh you look, [NON-ENGLISH]. From Warsaw? I say yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me how you found your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Lviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far-- how long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few hours during the night I went. I don't remember. Maybe five, six hours traveling. Traveling. I can't imagine the tales like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of belongings did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What belongings did you have with you from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. I had a good fur coat and a suitcase of changing clothes and underwear. And with that I travel. And I had a rucksack. And through-- through people that they knew me, told me where my brother is. And my brother came to see me. And I met a lady, a policewoman. Her husband was a-- was a policeman. She was a Gentile lady. She took me into her house, and took in my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there until I met my husband in the spring of 1940. Walking on the street, on the [NON-ENGLISH] street. I was walking alone, and I knew my husband when I was 14 years old, you know. And I didn't recognized me. He recognized me. And he ask me, aren't you from Warsaw? From that and that family? This and this and this?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stood and-- and turned around and say, you mean you know me? Because I didn't recognize him. I haven't seen him for so many years. How old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was 22. And I say, you recognize me that I am Towja Fiszer's daughter? He says, yes. And I'll tell you where you live, even.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I turn around, and he start talking. But my husband was not too far from Lviv. He was from Dubno, which is like four hours traveling by bus or car, whatever you went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And until that time. And then we decided that we're going to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cross the border, and got to Chernovitz from Lviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's stop here.
